quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Second derivative"?

options.text: If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) > 0: local minimum (concave up); f''(c) < 0: local maximum (concave down)
f''(x) = d²y/dx²; the derivative of the first derivative; measures how the slope itself is changing
0/0, ∞/∞, 0·∞, ∞-∞, 0⁰, 1^∞, ∞⁰; L'Hôpital's directly handles the first two
μ = E[X] = ∫₋∞^∞ x·f(x)dx; the 'center of mass' of the probability distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: "Second derivative" is defined as: f''(x) = d²y/dx²; the derivative of the first derivative; measures how the slope itself is changing. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Concave up"?

options.text: If f'(x) = 0 for all x in an interval, MVT proves f must be a constant function on that interval
Left: sample at left endpoint; Right: sample at right endpoint; Midpoint: sample at center of each subinterval
f''(x) > 0; the graph curves upward like a cup; the slope is increasing
The tolerance for how close f(x) must be to L; represents the desired precision of the output

quiz_questions.explanation: "Concave up" is defined as: f''(x) > 0; the graph curves upward like a cup; the slope is increasing. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Concave down"?

options.text: Product rule: two functions multiplied; Quotient rule: division; Chain rule: composition (function inside another function)
Divide the plate into thin horizontal strips of width dh; each strip has approximately constant pressure ρgh and area w(h)·dh
f''(x) < 0; the graph curves downward like a cap; the slope is decreasing
Uses the midpoint of each subinterval: M_n = Σf((xᵢ + xᵢ₊₁)/2)Δx; generally more accurate than left/right

quiz_questions.explanation: "Concave down" is defined as: f''(x) < 0; the graph curves downward like a cap; the slope is decreasing. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Inflection point"?

options.text: Two functions are asymptotically equivalent if lim(x→∞) f(x)/g(x) = 1
A point where concavity changes; f''(x) = 0 or undefined AND the sign of f''(x) changes
MVT + IVT: first use MVT to show f' achieves certain values, then use IVT on f' to show intermediate values exist
P = K: if the population is perturbed slightly above or below K, it returns to K

quiz_questions.explanation: "Inflection point" is defined as: A point where concavity changes; f''(x) = 0 or undefined AND the sign of f''(x) changes. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Higher-order derivatives"?

options.text: f'''(x), f⁴(x), etc.; in physics: position → velocity → acceleration → jerk → snap → crackle → pop
In economics: marginal cost C'(x), marginal revenue R'(x), and marginal profit P'(x) = R'(x) - C'(x) at production level x
[f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a); the average rate of change over [a,b]; the slope of the line connecting endpoints
Local min: concave up valley (f'' > 0); local max: concave down hill (f'' < 0)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Higher-order derivatives" is defined as: f'''(x), f⁴(x), etc.; in physics: position → velocity → acceleration → jerk → snap → crackle → pop. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f''(x) = d²y/dx²; the derivative of the first derivative; measures how the slope itself is changing"?

options.text: Limit does not exist
Second derivative
Linearity
Calculator vs. non-calculator

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Second derivative".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f''(x) > 0; the graph curves upward like a cup; the slope is increasing"?

options.text: Concave up
Integration by parts formula
When the test fails
Comparison with first derivative test

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Concave up".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f''(x) < 0; the graph curves downward like a cap; the slope is decreasing"?

options.text: Interval additivity
When the test fails
Restricting domain
Concave down

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Concave down".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A point where concavity changes; f''(x) = 0 or undefined AND the sign of f''(x) changes"?

options.text: Position, velocity, acceleration
Inflection point
Net change interpretation
Removable discontinuity

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Inflection point".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f'''(x), f⁴(x), etc.; in physics: position → velocity → acceleration → jerk → snap → crackle → pop"?

options.text: Notation
Derivative of tan x
Higher-order derivatives
Exponential function

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Higher-order derivatives".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity is accurate?

options.text: Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity extends differentiation beyond the first derivative.
Differential Equation Basics introduces equations involving derivatives.
Integration by parts may need to be applied more than once.
Choose u and dv from the integrand using the LIATE priority: Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential (choose u from earlier in the list).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity?

options.text: Linear approximation uses the tangent line to estimate function values near a known point: f(x) ≈ f(a) + f'(a)(x-a).
The second derivative f''(x) measures the rate of change of f'(x) and determines concavity: f''(x) > 0 means concave up (cup-shaped), f''(x) < 0 means concave down (cap-shaped).
Arc Length and Surface Area uses integration to measure curve length and rotational surface area.
Second Derivative Test provides a quick classification of critical points using concavity.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity?

options.text: This technique is essential for functions like xˣ, (sin x)^x, or x^(1/x) where both base and exponent are variable.
Inflection points occur where concavity changes (f''(x) = 0 or undefined, with sign change).
Trigonometric substitutions handle expressions with √(a²-x²), √(a²+x²), or √(x²-a²).
Common scenarios include expanding/contracting shapes, Pythagorean relationships, trigonometric relationships, and similar triangles.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity?

options.text: In physics, if s(t) is position, s'(t) is velocity, and s''(t) is acceleration—the 'jerk' is s'''(t).
Work against gravity: W = ∫mgy'(x)dx for lifting objects along curves.
If f'(x) > 0 on an interval, then f is increasing on that interval; if f'(x) < 0, f is decreasing.
This yields the derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions: d/dx[arcsin x] = 1/√(1-x²), d/dx[arccos x] = -1/√(1-x²), d/dx[arctan x] = 1/(1+x²).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity?

options.text: Integration by parts may need to be applied more than once.
Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity extends differentiation beyond the first derivative.
Combined with the chain rule: d/dx[e^(g(x))] = e^(g(x)) · g'(x) and d/dx[ln(g(x))] = g'(x)/g(x).
Arc length of y = f(x) from a to b: L = ∫ₐᵇ √(1 + [f'(x)]²)dx.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity?

options.text: Probability: the Cauchy distribution has CDF (1/π)arctan(x) + 1/2, obtained by integrating the probability density 1/(π(1+x²))
Financial mathematics: differentiating revenue functions R(x) to find marginal revenue and determine the optimal quantity to maximize profit
Calculating cable length for suspension bridges: the cable hangs in a catenary curve y = a·cosh(x/a), and arc length integration determines the required cable length
Acceleration as the second derivative of position: a car speeding up has positive s''(t), slowing down has negative s''(t), and constant speed has s''(t) = 0

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity?

options.text: Economics: the second derivative of profit with respect to quantity determines whether a critical point is a maximum (f''  0)
Computer algebra systems: software like Wolfram Alpha uses algorithmic decision trees similar to this strategy to compute symbolic integrals automatically
Finding the slope of a circle x² + y² = 25 at any point: dy/dx = -x/y, giving the tangent line direction for navigation and engineering applications
Navigation: calculating bearing angles using arctan(Δy/Δx) and differentiating to find rate of angle change for tracking systems

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Disease spread: the SIR model's infection curve resembles logistic growth, with the inflection point marking peak new infections
Oil spill spreading: a circular oil slick expanding on water has dA/dt = 2πr·dr/dt, used to estimate cleanup time and containment needs
Computing moments of inertia in physics: ∫x²·f(x)dx often requires integration by parts, essential for structural and mechanical engineering
Roller coaster design: inflection points (where concavity changes) create the thrilling transitions between upward and downward curves that riders feel

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity?

options.text: Acceleration as the second derivative of position: a car speeding up has positive s''(t), slowing down has negative s''(t), and constant speed has s''(t) = 0
Error propagation bounds: if a measuring instrument's response function has bounded derivative, MVT limits the maximum output error from a given input error
Probability: the Cauchy distribution has CDF (1/π)arctan(x) + 1/2, obtained by integrating the probability density 1/(π(1+x²))
Financial analysis: if an investment grows from $1000 to $1200 over a year, MVT guarantees the instantaneous growth rate was exactly 20% at some moment

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity in practice?

options.text: Rocket propulsion: as fuel is burned, the rocket becomes lighter, changing the force equation—integration handles the continuously varying mass
Oil spill spreading: a circular oil slick expanding on water has dA/dt = 2πr·dr/dt, used to estimate cleanup time and containment needs
Dam design: engineers compute total hydrostatic force on dam faces to determine required structural strength and materials
Economics: the second derivative of profit with respect to quantity determines whether a critical point is a maximum (f''  0)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Higher-Order Derivatives and Concavity.
